g.title: Final assembly views of the solar house
g.desc: Two diagrams show the completed house from the front and back. The structure is made of plywood panels with a slanted roof. A smooth, rectangular solar panel sits on top of the roof. The walls have windows made of four small square cutouts. You can feel where the pieces join by the small wooden tabs that protrude from slots along the corners and roofline. The front view shows a large open doorway on the left, while the back view shows a solid wall with multiple windows.

g.title: Solar House Kit Parts Inventory
g.desc: A list of 19 items needed for the project. Identify the wooden pieces by their 5mm thickness: one large roof panel, one small roof panel, one front wall, one back wall, and two side walls. Find the electronics: one flat solar panel with a smooth glass-like top, one cylindrical capacitor with two wire legs, and three small resistors with thin wire leads. The hardware includes four small hexagonal metal nuts and four threaded brass screws. Check that you have all these parts before you begin assembly.

g.title: Attach Solar Panel to Large Roof
g.desc: Identify the rectangular solar panel with two thin wires (red and black) and the large rectangular wooden roof piece. The roof piece has an oval-shaped hole in its center. First, thread the two wires from the back of the solar panel down through the oval hole in the roof. Next, take the two circular adhesive-backed velcro dots. Stick one dot to the center of the back of the solar panel and the other dot to the top surface of the roof piece, right next to the oval hole. Finally, press the solar panel onto the roof piece so the two velcro dots grip each other.

g.title: Routing Solar Panel Wires through Wood Plate
g.desc: Identify the small rectangular solar panel and the rectangular plywood plate. The wooden plate can be identified by its large circular hole and a smaller horizontal oval slot located near its center. To assemble, take the red and black wires attached to the solar panel and feed their pin-ended tips through the horizontal oval slot in the plywood plate from the top side.

g.title: Assembling house walls using notches and slots
g.desc: Connect three plywood pieces to form the house walls. The main wall is a long piece with a sloped top edge and two vertical slots on both its left and right sides. Take the doorway panel, which has a large rectangular hole, and locate the two hook-shaped tabs on its side. Tilt this panel to slide its tabs into the slots on the left side of the main wall. Similarly, take the side wall panel with window cutouts and slide its hook-shaped tabs into the slots on the right side of the main wall.

g.title: Attaching the front arch wall frame
g.desc: This step involves connecting a large, peaked arch frame to the front of the two already joined side walls. The side walls can be identified by their square window cutouts and the protruding rectangular tabs on their front edges. The front arch is a single piece shaped like an inverted 'U' with a pointed top and two vertical slots on each of its legs. Align the four tabs on the side walls with the four corresponding slots on the arch piece, then slide them together until they fit snugly.

g.title: Attaching Roof Pieces and Mounting to Breadboard
g.desc: Place the house frame on top of the rectangular white breadboard. You will attach two roof pieces to the frame's sloped top edges: one wide rectangular panel and one narrow strip. Identify the T-shaped slots on the top edges of the frame walls. To fasten the roof, slide a brass hex nut into the horizontal crossbar of each T-slot. Lay the roof pieces on the frame, aligning their holes with the vertical part of the 'T', and secure them by driving a screw through the hole and into the nut. Repeat this for all four mounting points.

g.title: Attach Screw to Threaded Insert
g.desc: This diagram shows how to connect a 5mm thick plywood piece using a #4-40 brass screw and a brass threaded insert. First, find a flat, smooth piece of 5mm thick plywood that has a single round hole through its center. Next, locate a #4-40 brass screw; you can identify it by its round head with a screwdriver slot and its distinct spiral threads. Then, find the brass threaded insert, a small, hollow brass component that feels cylindrical or barrel-shaped. It has a rounded top, a slightly narrower middle, and a slightly wider base, with a threaded hole running through its center. To assemble, push the screw through the hole in the plywood from one side. Then, align the tip of the screw with the threaded opening of the brass insert. Turn the screw clockwise until it is fully tightened and the screw head is snug against the plywood surface.

g.title: Completed Wooden Greenhouse Structure
g.desc: The final assembly of the greenhouse is a rectangular wooden structure with a slanted roof. You can identify the flat plywood panels that form the walls and the roof. The roof contains a large, smooth, rectangular black screen. The walls feature several square window cutouts. The entire structure is held together by interlocking tabs and slots at the corners, secured with brass screws and nuts in the cross-shaped T-slots located near the joins. This indicates that all components are now securely attached to each other.
